mnemonics.title: FRINRQ - Functional Requirements INclude
mnemonics.content: Functional Requirements INclude: Features, Reporting, Input/Output, Navigation, Queries - these define what the system DOES.

mnemonics.title: NFR SPARES - Non-Functional Requirements
mnemonics.content: Non-Functional Requirements: Security, Performance, Availability, Reliability, Extensibility, Scalability - these define how the system PERFORMS.

mnemonics.title: CAPACITY Planning Steps
mnemonics.content: CAPACITY: Collect baseline data, Analyze workload, Plan resources, Assess current capacity, Test scenarios, Update regularly, Execute changes, Validate results.

mnemonics.title: UTILITY Metrics
mnemonics.content: UTILITY helps remember key metrics: Utilization (CPU/Memory), Throughput, Error rates, Latency, Load time, Yield (success rate), I/O operations.

acronyms.usage: Contract defining expected service performance metrics between provider and customer.
Measurable value demonstrating effectiveness in achieving business objectives.
Maximum acceptable time to restore service after disruption.
Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time before failure.
Throughput metric measuring system processing capacity.
Measure of overall performance of a service or network.
System design approach ensuring minimal downtime.

visualTips.visualization: Think of CPU as a factory worker - utilization is percentage of time worker is actively producing output versus standing idle.
Throughput is the assembly line speed (cars per hour); Response time is how long ONE car takes from start to finish.
Vertical scaling = upgrading one car to have bigger engine; Horizontal scaling = adding more cars to the fleet.
Think of 99% as almost always available (8,760 hours = 1 year; 99% means only ~87.6 hours downtime = ~3.6 days).
Memory is like workspace on your desk - utilization shows how much of your desk is covered with papers you're working on.

analogies.analogy: Functional is the recipe ingredients (what goes in); Non-Functional is how the dish should taste, look, and be served (quality of outcome).
Like taking your temperature when healthy - it's your baseline normal, so you know when you have a fever.
Like a restaurant owner predicting how many tables and staff needed during holiday seasons based on past data.
Like a hospital categorizing patients (emergency, scheduled appointment, routine checkup) to allocate appropriate resources.
A small bookshop expanding to a warehouse store during peak (adding space/staff) versus having multiple small stores across city.
